I need to examine the `close_if_unusable_or_obsolete` function more carefully to identify where the issue is.\n\nLooking at the condition around line 614, I'm checking how `CONN_MAX_AGE` affects the close logic. When `CONN_MAX_AGE = 0`, the `close_at` timestamp is set to the current time at connection creation, so the condition `time.monotonic() >= self.close_at` immediately evaluates to true and closes the connection. With `CONN_MAX_AGE = None`, `close_at` remains `None`, so the check `None is not None` fails and the connection stays open. The real issue might be with the comparison operator—if it were `>` instead of `>=`, connections with `CONN_MAX_AGE = 0` would never actually close since the current time would equal `close_at` at the moment of creation.\n\nLet me check the git history for this repository to see what changed.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":509,"cache_read_input_tokens":39131,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":509,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":8,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"2cf45a69-5887-4766-896d-6c64f30a9ae1","uuid":"26cd3e79-8795-4398-b08e-541439625b8f"}

Anyone can help by working on tickets, triaging tickets and\\n+    reviewing changes. Django is a community project, and we encourage\\n+    :ref:`triage and collaboration by the community\\n+    <how-can-i-help-with-triaging>`. We truly want you to help!\\n \\n For example, here's the typical lifecycle of a ticket:\\n \\n\",\"newRange\":{\"lines\":19,\"startLine\":63},\"oldRange\":{\"lines\":15,\"startLine\":63},\"section\":\"vote of the :ref:`Steering Council <steering-council>`.\"},{\"body\":\" * Please **don't** promote your own tickets to \\\"Ready for checkin\\\". When someone writes a fix for the bug, they'll already\\n have your test as a starting point.\\n+\\n+.. _reviewing-patches:\\n+\\n+Reviewing patches\\n+=================\\n+\\n+A great way to learn the Django codebase is to help review patches. It allows\\n+you to focus on a fixed scope of Django with a particular perspective. Plus,\\n+Django tends to receive more patches than it can review which causes the\\n+`review queue`_ to build up. Contributors can help Django tremendously by\\n+reviewing patches.\\n+\\n+The `review queue`_ is defined by tickets in Trac_ that are in the \\\"Accepted\\\"\\n+state with the flag \\\"has patch\\\" set and the flags \\\"patch needs improvement\\\",\\n+\\\"needs tests\\\" and \\\"needs documentation\\\" unset. There is also a set of\\n+`Pull Requests in GitHub with the \\\"no ticket\\\" label`_ that can be reviewed.\\n+\\n+Code review process\\n+-------------------\\n+\\n+The general process to reviewing a patch for Django is as follows:\\n+\\n+1. Pick a patch to review. This can be from the `review queue`_ or the\\n+   `Pull Requests in GitHub with the \\\"no ticket\\\" label`_. If you're new, pick\\n+   a specific component to stick with. Django is a big codebase, so it's best\\n+   to contribute consistently with a narrow focus and grow it slowly.\\n+\\n+2. Pull the branch. See the :ref:`handling-pull-requests`, including the git\\n+   alias to make checking out other pull requests easier.\\n+\\n+3. Check the tests. If the ticket is for a bug, there should be a regression\\n+   test. Confirm that the test fails if the change is reverted. Check that the\\n+   tests are adequately covering the code and logic as well. See\\n+   :ref:`patch-review-checklist`.\\n+\\n+4. Test in your own Django project. See :ref:`installing-development-version`\\n+   for how. Explore the user interface and user experience with the change.\\n+   Does it feel right? Can you break it? Another approach is to assume\\n+   everything is wrong. Can you demonstrate to yourself that each piece is\\n+   correct?\\n+\\n+5. Check the documentation. Is the documentation understandable? Does it\\n+   provide the information the reader needs at that moment? Does it read well?\\n+   Is it consistent with the documentation before and after it? See\\n+   :ref:`build-documentation-locally`.\\n+\\n+6. Are you happy with the patch? This is fairly subjective, but that's fine.\\n+   You should ask, does the approach make sense to you and is there another\\n+   way to do it that's better? Also ask yourself, is the scope of the change\\n+   appropriate? Are things named appropriately? Renaming things is slow and\\n+   challenging in Django, so we should try to get it right from the start.\\n+   Are there backwards compatibility concerns? Lastly, are there any other\\n+   general concerns such as things that may worry you or that should be looked\\n+   into?\\n+\\n+7. Review your feedback. Before you submit your feedback, consider the\\n+   feedback you want to share. It takes courage to open a Pull Request, w\\n... It allows\\n+you to focus on a fixed scope of Django with a particular perspective. Plus,\\n+Django tends to receive more patches than it can review which causes the\\n+`review queue`_ to build up. Contributors can help Django tremendously by\\n+reviewing patches.\\n+\\n+The `review queue`_ is defined by tickets in Trac_ that are in the \\\"Accepted\\\"\\n+state with the flag \\\"has patch\\\" set and the flags \\\"patch needs improvement\\\",\\n+\\\"needs tests\\\" and \\\"needs documentation\\\" unset. There is also a set of\\n+`Pull Requests in GitHub with the \\\"no ticket\\\" label`_ that can be reviewed.\\n+\\n+Code review process\\n+-------------------\\n+\\n+The general process to reviewing a patch for Django is as follows:\\n+\\n+1. Pick a patch to review. This can be from the `review queue`_ or the\\n+   `Pull Requests in GitHub with the \\\"no ticket\\\" label`_. If you're new, pick\\n+   a specific component to stick with. Django is a big codebase, so it's best\\n+   to contribute consistently with a narrow focus and grow it slowly.\\n+\\n+2. Pull the branch. See the :ref:`handling-pull-requests`, including the git\\n+   alias to make checking out other pull requests easier.\\n+\\n+3. Check the tests. If the ticket is for a bug, there should be a regression\\n+   test. Confirm that the test fails if the change is reverted. Check that the\\n+   tests are adequately covering the code and logic as well. See\\n+   :ref:`patch-review-checklist`.\\n+\\n+4. Test in your own Django project. See :ref:`installing-development-version`\\n+   for how. Explore the user interface and user experience with the change.\\n+   Does it feel right? Can you break it? Another approach is to assume\\n+   everything is wrong. Can you demonstrate to yourself that each piece is\\n+   correct?\\n+\\n+5. Check the documentation. Is the documentation understandable? Does it\\n+   provide the information the reader needs at that moment? Does it read well?\\n+   Is it consistent with the documentation before and after it? See\\n+   :ref:`build-documentation-locally`.\\n+\\n+6. Are you happy with the patch? This is fairly subjective, but that's fine.\\n+   You should ask, does the approach make sense to you and is there another\\n+   way to do it that's better? Also ask yourself, is the scope of the change\\n+   appropriate? Are things named appropriately? Renaming things is slow and\\n+   challenging in Django, so we should try to get it right from the start.\\n+   Are there backwards compatibility concerns? Lastly, are there any other\\n+   general concerns such as things that may worry you or that should be looked\\n+   into?\\n+\\n+7. Review your feedback. Before you submit your feedback, consider the\\n+   feedback you want to share. It takes courage to open a Pull Request, w\\n... When someone writes a fix for the bug, they'll already\n have your test as a starting point.\n+\n+.. _reviewing-patches:\n+\n+Reviewing patches\n+=================\n+\n+A great way to learn the Django codebase is to help review patches. It allows\n+you to focus on a fixed scope of Django with a particular perspective. Plus,\n+Django tends to receive more patches than it can review which causes the\n+`review queue`_ to build up. Contributors can help Django tremendously by\n+reviewing patches.\n+\n+The `review queue`_ is defined by tickets in Trac_ that are in the \"Accepted\"\n+state with the flag \"has patch\" set and the flags \"patch needs improvement\",\n+\"needs tests\" and \"needs documentation\" unset. There is also a set of\n+`Pull Requests in GitHub with the \"no ticket\" label`_ that can be reviewed.\n+\n+Code review process\n+-------------------\n+\n+The general process to reviewing a patch for Django is as follows:\n+\n+1. Pick a patch to review. This can be from the `review queue`_ or the\n+   `Pull Requests in GitHub with the \"no ticket\" label`_. If you're new, pick\n+   a specific component to stick with. Django is a big codebase, so it's best\n+   to contribute consistently with a narrow focus and grow it slowly.\n+\n+2. Pull the branch. See the :ref:`handling-pull-requests`, including the git\n+   alias to make checking out other pull requests easier.\n+\n+3. Check the tests. If the ticket is for a bug, there should be a regression\n+   test. Confirm that the test fails if the change is reverted. Check that the\n+   tests are adequately covering the code and logic as well. See\n+   :ref:`patch-review-checklist`.\n+\n+4. Test in your own Django project. See :ref:`installing-development-version`\n+   for how. Explore the user interface and user experience with the change.\n+   Does it feel right? Can you break it? Another approach is to assume\n+   everything is wrong. Can you demonstrate to yourself that each piece is\n+   correct?\n+\n+5. Check the documentation. Is the documentation understandable? Does it\n+   provide the information the reader needs at that moment? Does it read well?\n+   Is it consistent with the documentation before and after it? See\n+   :ref:`build-documentation-locally`.\n+\n+6. Are you happy with the patch? This is fairly subjective, but that's fine.\n+   You should ask, does the approach make sense to you and is there another\n+   way to do it that's better? Also ask yourself, is the scope of the change\n+   appropriate? Are things named appropriately? Renaming things is slow and\n+   challenging in Django, so we should try to get it right from the start.\n+   Are there backwards compatibility concerns? Lastly, are there any other\n+   general concerns such as things that may worry you or that should be looked\n+   into?\n+\n+7. Review your feedback. Before you submit your feedback, consider the\n+   feedback you want to share. It takes courage to open a Pull Request, w\n... To avoid this buggy behavior and to actually enter a new\\n466:         transaction, an explicit BEGIN is required. Using\\n467:         force_begin_transaction_with_broken_autocommit=True will issue an\\n468:         explicit BEGIN with SQLite. This option will be ignored for other\\n469:         backends.\\n470:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n471:         self.validate_no_atomic_block()\\n472:         self.close_if_health_check_failed()\\n473: If the application didn't restore the original autocommit setting,\n            # don't take chances, drop the connection.\n            if self.get_autocommit() != self.settings_dict[\"AUTOCOMMIT\"]:\n                self.close()\n                return\n\n            # If an exception other than DataError or IntegrityError occurred\n            # since the last commit / rollback, check if the connection works.\n            if self.errors_occurred:\n                if self.is_usable():\n                    self.errors_occurred = False\n                    self.health_check_done = True\n                else:\n                    self.close()\n                    return\n\n            if self.close_at is not None and time.monotonic() >= self.close_at:\n                self.close()\n                return\n\n    # Looking at the three conditions that trigger closure, with `CONN_MAX_AGE=None` the autocommit check should be fine, no errors should have occurred, and `close_at` should be `None` so the timestamp check should be false. This suggests something is setting `close_at` to a non-None value when it shouldn't be.\n\nThe code shows `self.close_at = None if max_age is None else time.monotonic() + max_age`, which looks correct, but maybe there's a logic issue with how `max_age` is being evaluated. Let me actually run the test to see what's failing rather than just reasoning through it.\n\nSearching for \"CONN_MAX_AGE\" references in the sg-evals repo to understand how this setting is being used.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":3500,"cache_read_input_tokens":15269,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":3500,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":8,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"2cf45a69-5887-4766-896d-6c64f30a9ae1","uuid":"0396b874-dda4-4d99-9b6d-52e6c5d2a10b"}

All test cases need newly configured and created connections.\n        connection.close()\n\n    def tearDown(self):\n        connection.close()\n\n    def patch_settings_dict(self, conn_health_checks):\n        self.settings_dict_patcher = patch.dict(\n            connection.settings_dict,\n            {\n                **connection.settings_dict,\n                \"CONN_MAX_AGE\": None,\n                \"CONN_HEALTH_CHECKS\": conn_health_checks,\n            },\n        )\n        self.settings_dict_patcher.start()\n        self.addCleanup(self.settings_dict_patcher.stop)\n\n    def run_query(self):\n        with connection.cursor() as cursor:\n            cursor.execute(\"SELECT 42\")\n\n    def test_health_checks_enabled(self):\n        \"\"\"CONN_MAX_AGE=None connections should not be closed by close_if_unusable_or_obsolete.\"\"\"\n        self.patch_settings_dict(conn_health_checks=True)\n        self.assertIsNone(connection.connection)\n\n        # Newly created connections are considered healthy without performing health check.\n        with patch.object(connection, \"is_usable\", side_effect=AssertionError):\n            self.run_query()\n\n        old_connection = connection.connection\n        # Simulate request_finished.\n        connection.close_if_unusable_or_obsolete()\n        # CONN_MAX_AGE=None: connection should NOT be closed\n        self.assertIs(old_connection, connection.connection,\n                      f\"FAIL: persistent connection (CONN_MAX_AGE=None) was closed! \"\n                      f\"close_at={connection.close_at}\")\n\n    def test_health_checks_disabled(self):\n        \"\"\"CONN_MAX_AGE=None with health checks disabled: connection should persist.\"\"\"\n        self.patch_settings_dict(conn_health_checks=False)\n        self.assertIsNone(connection.connection)\n\n        # Newly created connections are considered healthy without performing health check.\n        with patch.object(connection, \"is_usable\", side_effect=AssertionError):\n            self.run_query()\n\n        old_connection = connection.connection\n        # {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","id":"msg_014zrQF2VvVmL9G3hsYSakX7","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"OK so the sg-evals SQLite `close()` is the same as installed Django. Both skip closing for in-memory databases. This is why my tests with `:memory:` SQLite were failing.\n\nNow let me think about what the ACTUAL bug could be. The tests in the sg-evals repo use `@skipUnlessDBFeature(\"test_db_allows_multiple_connections\")`. For SQLite, this would typically skip in-memory databases.
